question: Two programs do the exact same job, but one finishes in a blink and the other takes an age. In your own words, why can the SAME job take such wildly different amounts of time — and how do you pick the tool that makes it fast?
guidance: You've measured this all class long — flat lines, climbing lines, steep hills, and curves that dive. Explain what Big-O really means, and give an example or two from your own code where picking a better tool (or structure) made the work shrink.
lookFor: Speed isn't about the computer being fast — it's about HOW MANY STEPS the method takes as the input grows (Big-O)
Different shapes grow differently: O(1) stays flat, O(log n) barely rises, O(n) climbs straight, O(n²) curves up steeply — so for big inputs the choice matters enormously

Picking the right tool/structure changes the shape: binary search (O(log n)) beats linear search (O(n)) but needs a sorted list; merge sort (O(n log n)) beats bubble sort (O(n²)); a hash table jumps straight to the answer (O(1)); a BST keeps search to one path down
A concrete example from their own work where counting the steps or choosing a structure made the job much smaller

A connection to another class: DNS caching skips a slow lookup (Networks), each extra password digit multiplies the cracking work by 10 (White Hat), or the scheduler decides whose turn is next (Operating Systems)
exemplar: The computer doesn't magically get faster — what changes is how many STEPS your method takes. That's what Big-O measures: not how long one step is, but how the number of steps grows as the job gets bigger. If a method is O(1) it's a flat line — same cost forever, like grabbing the first box or jumping straight to a hash-table bucket. O(log n) barely rises, because each step throws away half the work, like binary search or walking one path down a search tree. O(n) climbs in a straight line — you touch everything once, like linear search. And O(n²) curves up into a steep hill, like bubble sort, where doubling the list quadruples the swaps. For small inputs nobody cares, but for a million items the difference is a blink versus an afternoon. So to make a job fast, I pick the tool with the gentler curve: sort the list so I can binary-search it, use merge sort instead of bubble sort, drop things in a hash table so lookups are instant. I saw this everywhere — DNS caching in Networks skips the slow lookup the second time, and in White Hat every extra digit in a password multiplied the cracking work by ten. Speed is really just choosing the shape of the curve.
